info.title: auth-app API
info.description: The JSON API the admin frontend consumes.

get.summary:
Handle `GET /audit/events?limit=`: the most recent audit events, newest
first, capped at [`MAX_LIMIT`] regardless of what the caller asks for.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Maximum number of events to return (default 50, capped at 200).

403.description: Authenticated but not the admin role

post.description:
`200` with the admin id and fresh session/CSRF cookies on success; `401`
for invalid credentials (identical for a wrong password and a nonexistent
account); `429` with `Retry-After` when the IP or account has hit the
app-level rate limit, or when the account/IP is locked out (see
[`LoginError::TooManyAttempts`] — a distinct mechanism from the rate limit:
lockout counts *failures*, the rate limit counts *every* attempt); `500` on
an internal failure. Every successful login issues a brand-new session —
there is no "reuse the prior session" path — which is what satisfies
session rotation on login.

200.description: Signed in; sets session and csrf cookies

401.description: Invalid credentials (wrong password or unknown account)

429.description: Rate-limited or locked out; carries Retry-After

post.summary:
Handle `POST /auth/logout`: revoke the session server-side, record the
event to the audit trail, and clear both cookies. Requires (via
[`require_session`]) a valid session and a matching CSRF header — logout
is a mutation like any other.

204.description: Logged out; clears the session and csrf cookies

get.summary:
Handle `GET /auth/me`: report the authenticated admin's id and role, so
frontend guards can hide routes/actions the role does not permit without
a separate round trip. Any authenticated role may call this — it is not
itself role-gated (see [`crate::rbac::require_role`] for endpoints that
are).

200.description: The authenticated admin's id and role

get.summary: Readiness probe: `200 OK` when ready, `503 Service Unavailable` otherwise.
get.description:
Awaits the application service, which in turn probes the live database, so a
down or unreachable Postgres surfaces here as `503`.

EventOut.description: One audit event, as returned to the admin panel.

admin_id.description: The resolved admin id, when the event maps to a known account.

email_attempted.description:
The email submitted, for login events (kept even when it matched no
account).

ip.description: The client IP the request came from.

occurred_at_epoch.description: When the event happened, as Unix epoch seconds.

LoginBody.description: The JSON body of a login request.

LoginOk.description: The success body: the authenticated administrator's id.

MeOut.description:
The body `GET /auth/me` returns: enough for the frontend to know who is
logged in and which role's guards to apply.
